trm:term.trm:name: Python Binding
trm:term.trm:definition: A PyO3-based native extension that wraps the Rust clayers crates as a Python package. The binding translates Rust types into Python classes, maps Rust errors to a typed exception hierarchy, and bridges the async/sync divide between the tokio-based clayers-repo and Python's asyncio. Published as the "clayers" package on PyPI.

trm:term.trm:name: Knowledge Model
trm:term.trm:definition: The Python class that represents a loaded clayers specification. Wraps spec discovery, validation, drift detection, coverage analysis, connectivity analysis, hash fixing, and XPath querying behind a single stateful object. Constructed from a filesystem path pointing to a spec directory.

trm:term.trm:name: Queryable Protocol
trm:term.trm:definition: A Python typing.Protocol that both KnowledgeModel and Repo implement, defining a common query(xpath, mode) interface. Enables generic Python functions to accept either a spec or a repository as a query target, unifying the two query surfaces.

trm:term.trm:name: RepoInner
trm:term.trm:definition: An enum-dispatch wrapper around the generic Repo<S> type, since PyO3 does not support generic pyclasses. RepoInner holds either Repo<MemoryStore> or Repo<SqliteStore>, wrapped in Arc for sharing between sync and async Python classes. A dispatch macro routes method calls to the correct variant.

trm:term.trm:name: Global Tokio Runtime
trm:term.trm:definition: A lazily-initialized, shared tokio multi-thread runtime used by the sync Repo class to bridge from synchronous Python calls to the async clayers-repo methods. Created once via OnceLock and reused across all sync Repo instances. The sync class releases the Python GIL via Python.detach() before calling block_on(), preventing deadlocks between the GIL and the tokio runtime.

pr:section.pr:title: Python Integration
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: PyO3 bindings exposing the clayers crates as a native Python package with both sync and async APIs.
pr:section.pr:p: The clayers-py crate provides Python bindings to all four Rust crates: clayers-xml (content hashing, canonicalization), clayers-spec (validation, drift, coverage, connectivity, querying), clayers-repo (version-controlled XML repositories), and the CLI-level assembly logic. The result is a single Python package, import clayers, that enables Python tooling to work with specs and repositories natively without shelling out to the CLI.
The binding is built with PyO3 and packaged via maturin. The native extension module is clayers._clayers, and pure Python wrapper modules provide re-exports, protocols, and submodule structure.

Typical use cases include: CI pipelines that validate specs and fail on drift, pytest suites that assert coverage thresholds, Jupyter notebooks that explore spec connectivity, web dashboards that render spec health metrics, and agent frameworks that query specs programmatically instead of parsing CLI output.

pr:sidebar.pr:title: Package boundary
pr:sidebar.pr:p: The Python package is a native integration surface, not a wrapper around subprocess calls. It exposes the same spec, repository, and query concepts as the Rust crates.

pr:section.pr:title: Module Structure
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: How the Python package is organized into submodules and re-exports.
pr:section.pr:p: The package follows a layered structure mirroring the Rust crates:

Users switch between sync and async by changing the import path. The sync from clayers.repo import Repo and async from clayers.repo.aio import Repo share the same class name and method signatures, making them a drop-in swap.

pr:section.pr:title: KnowledgeModel API
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: The central class for working with clayers specs from Python.
pr:section.pr:p: The KnowledgeModel class is constructed from a filesystem path and eagerly discovers index files and spec files. Each method returns a typed result object that mirrors the corresponding Rust struct with read-only Python attributes. Errors are raised as SpecError exceptions.
pr:section.pr:synopsis: from clayers import KnowledgeModel km = KnowledgeModel("clayers/my-project")

py:doc.pr:p: Compare stored hashes against current content.
Graph metrics: components, hubs, bridges, cycles.
Recompute spec-side hashes in artifact mappings.
Recompute code-side hashes in artifact mappings.

pr:example.pr:title: CI gate for validation and drift
pr:example.pr:codeblock:
from clayers import KnowledgeModel
km = KnowledgeModel("clayers/my-project") result = km.validate() assert result.is_valid, [e.message for e in result.errors]
drift = km.check_drift() assert drift.drifted_count == 0, ( f"{drift.drifted_count} mappings drifted" )

pr:example.pr:title: Query the spec as a knowledge base
pr:example.pr:codeblock:
km = KnowledgeModel("clayers/clayers")
# Find all terms terms = km.query("//trm:term/trm:name", mode="text") print(terms.values) # ['Layer', 'Namespace', 'Node', ...]
# Find artifact mappings for a concept paths = km.query( '//art:mapping[art:spec-ref/@node="term-drift"]' '/art:artifact/@path', mode="text", ) print(paths.values) # ['crates/clayers-spec/src/drift.rs']

pr:example.pr:title: Coverage threshold test in pytest
pr:example.pr:codeblock: def test_spec_coverage_above_threshold(): km = KnowledgeModel("clayers/my-project") cov = km.coverage() mapped_pct = cov.mapped_nodes / cov.total_nodes * 100 assert mapped_pct >= 80, ( f"Only {mapped_pct:.0f}% of spec nodes are mapped. " f"Unmapped: {cov.unmapped_nodes}" )

pr:example.pr:title: Explore connectivity in a notebook
pr:example.pr:codeblock: km = KnowledgeModel("clayers/clayers") conn = km.connectivity() print(f"Nodes: {conn.node_count}, Edges: {conn.edge_count}") print(f"Density: {conn.density:.4f}") for hub in conn.hub_nodes[:3]: print(f" Hub: {hub.id} (degree {hub.total_degree})") for node_id in conn.isolated_nodes[:5]: print(f" Isolated: {node_id}")

pr:section.pr:title: Repository API
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: Sync and async Python wrappers for the content-addressed XML repository.
pr:section.pr:p: The repository API wraps clayers-repo's Repo<S> behind RepoInner enum dispatch. Both the sync and async Repo classes hold an Arc<RepoInner> and expose identical method signatures. The sync class uses a shared tokio runtime with block_on() after releasing the GIL. The async class returns Python awaitables via future_into_py().

py:doc.pr:p: Decompose XML into the Merkle DAG, return document hash.
Reconstruct XML from its content hash.
Create commit on branch pointing to tree.

pr:section.pr:p: A version-controlled XML editing workflow using the sync API:
pr:section.pr:codeblock:
from clayers.repo import Repo, MemoryStore, Author
repo = Repo(MemoryStore()) author = Author("Alice", "alice@example.com")
# Import documents and commit spec = repo.import_xml("<spec><section id='intro'/></spec>") index = repo.import_xml("<index><file href='spec.xml'/></index>") tree = repo.build_tree([ ("spec.xml", spec), ("index.xml", index), ]) commit = repo.commit("main", tree, author, "Initial spec")
# Later: modify, commit, and diff spec_v2 = repo.import_xml("<spec><section id='intro' status='draft'/></spec>") tree_v2 = repo.build_tree([("spec.xml", spec_v2), ("index.xml", index)]) commit_v2 = repo.commit("main", tree_v2, author, "Add draft status")
changes = repo.diff_trees(tree, tree_v2) for c in changes: print(f"{c.kind}: {c.path}")
pr:section.pr:p: The same workflow using the async API (only the import changes):
pr:section.pr:codeblock:
from clayers.repo.aio import Repo # only this line changes from clayers.repo import MemoryStore, Author
repo = Repo(MemoryStore()) author = Author("Alice", "alice@example.com")
spec = await repo.import_xml("<spec/>") tree = await repo.build_tree([("spec.xml", spec)]) commit = await repo.commit("main", tree, author, "Initial")
pr:section.pr:p: Persistent storage with SQLite survives process restarts:
pr:section.pr:codeblock:
from clayers.repo import Repo, Author from clayers import SqliteStore
repo = Repo(SqliteStore.open("my-project.db")) # All commits, branches, tags persist to disk

pr:section.pr:title: Repository Leaf Hashing
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: Non-text leaf node hashes use separate domains so distinct node types cannot alias text payloads.
pr:section.pr:p: The repository import pipeline decomposes XML into a content-addressed Merkle DAG. Text, comment, and processing-instruction leaves are stored as distinct object variants, so their identity hashes must not alias across node kinds when payload bytes match. Text leaves keep the legacy raw-payload hash domain for repository compatibility; comment and processing-instruction leaves use explicit non-text domains. A text node with content x and a comment node with content x therefore have different hashes and must round-trip through import, storage, and export without either leaf replacing the other.

The non-text leaf encoding is a byte sequence that starts with the ASCII prefix clayers-repo:leaf:v1\0, followed by the kind tag (comment or pi), a NUL separator, and then each payload part as an unsigned 64-bit big-endian length followed by that part's bytes. Comment leaves have one payload part: the comment text. Processing-instruction leaves have two payload parts: target and data, with missing data encoded as the empty byte string. Length-prefixing means adjacent parts cannot be reinterpreted as a different target/data split.

Non-text leaf domain separation preserves the Merkle invariant that a hash identifies exactly one object identity while avoiding a broad migration for existing text-heavy repositories. It also keeps transaction stores safe to implement idempotent insert or replace semantics: replacing an object at the same hash is valid only when the object identity is the same, not when two XML node kinds happen to share payload bytes.

pr:section.pr:title: Repository Working Copy Safety
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: Checkout and pull refuse to overwrite local working-copy edits.
pr:section.pr:p: The CLI repository commands keep a working_copy table that records the document hash last written for each tracked file. Any operation that rewrites the working tree from repository objects, such as checkout or pull, must compare tracked files on disk with those recorded hashes before removing or overwriting files. Staged changes, unstaged tracked modifications, and unstaged tracked deletions are local work and must cause the command to fail before the working tree is rewritten.

The export step must also protect untracked files at paths that the target tree would write or remove. If a path exists on disk but is not recorded as a clean tracked file in working_copy, the export must fail instead of replacing it with repository content. Clone and first pull into an empty directory can still write new files because no existing local file is being replaced.

Pull must run the dirty-working-copy guard before synchronizing remote refs, then preflight the remote current-branch tree against local target paths before calling ref synchronization. A rejected pull therefore does not advance local branch refs while leaving the disk on the old content. Checkout must run the same dirty-working-copy guard before creating or switching branches. These checks make branch movement fail-fast and preserve the user's local edits.

Export must not silently degrade when reading repository state for the previous branch fails. Old-tree lookup errors are propagated so stale tracked files are not left behind by treating the old tree as empty. The target-path safety check uses async file-system inspection and reads when running inside async repository export operations, so it does not block the executor while comparing local XML against the tracked object hash.

pr:section.pr:title: Repository Working Copy Observation
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: Status and working-copy diff report every local XML file state relative to the tracked working copy.
pr:section.pr:p: The CLI repository status and no-argument diff commands observe the on-disk working copy against the working_copy table. They must consider both sides of that comparison: tracked paths recorded in the table and XML files currently present on disk. A tracked path that is missing from disk is a local deletion, even if it was deleted manually rather than through clayers rm.

Status reports unstaged tracked deletions alongside unstaged tracked modifications, and reports XML files that exist on disk but are not tracked as untracked. No-argument diff reports working-copy changes relative to the current HEAD: tracked deletions as deleted files, tracked content changes as modified files with structural XML differences when available, and untracked XML files as added files. Clean output is reserved for the case where no tracked path changed and no untracked XML file exists. A single invocation may report multiple working-copy states at once, such as a manually deleted tracked file and a newly added untracked XML file.

pr:section.pr:title: Error Hierarchy
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: How Rust errors map to Python exceptions.
pr:section.pr:p: A base ClayersError exception inherits from Python's Exception. Three subclasses map to the Rust crate error types:

py:doc.pr:p: Base exception for all clayers errors.
Raised by KnowledgeModel constructor and all methods.
Raised by Repo constructor and all methods.

pr:section.pr:p: Catching errors at different levels of specificity:

pr:section.pr:title: Shared Query Protocol
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: How KnowledgeModel and Repo share the Queryable interface.
pr:section.pr:p: Both KnowledgeModel and Repo implement the Queryable protocol, a runtime-checkable typing.Protocol. This enables generic Python code to accept either as a query target.
Writing a function that works with both filesystem specs and versioned repositories:
pr:section.pr:codeblock:
from clayers import Queryable
def count_terms(source: Queryable) -> int: result = source.query("//trm:term", mode="count") return result.count or 0
# Works with specs on disk from clayers import KnowledgeModel km = KnowledgeModel("clayers/my-project") print(count_terms(km)) # e.g. 42
# Works identically with a repository snapshot from clayers.repo import Repo, MemoryStore repo = Repo(MemoryStore()) # ... after importing and committing documents ... print(count_terms(repo))

org:concept.org:purpose: Understanding the purpose and scope of the Python bindings and how they relate to the four Rust crates.

org:reference.org:purpose: Lookup reference for which Python module wraps which Rust crate and what types are available.
Reference for the KnowledgeModel class methods and return types.
Reference for the sync/async Repo API and the inner dispatch design.

org:concept.org:purpose: Understanding object identity for leaf nodes in the content-addressed repository DAG.
Understanding safety checks that prevent checkout and pull from overwriting local working-copy edits.
Understanding how status and working-copy diff classify tracked deletions, tracked modifications, and untracked XML files.
Understanding how Rust errors propagate as typed Python exceptions.
Understanding the shared query protocol and QueryResult type.
Core vocabulary for the Python integration.
The central Python class wrapping spec operations.
The shared query interface between KnowledgeModel and Repo.
The enum dispatch pattern for wrapping generic Rust types in PyO3.
The shared tokio runtime bridging sync Python to async Rust.

rel:relation.rel:note: The Python bindings expose the layered architecture to Python tooling, wrapping all four Rust crates.
KnowledgeModel assembles the combined document to answer queries.
KnowledgeModel.check_drift() delegates to drift detection.
KnowledgeModel.coverage() delegates to coverage analysis.
KnowledgeModel.fix_artifact_hashes() and fix_node_hashes() operate on artifact mappings.
Both sync and async Repo hold Arc<RepoInner>.
The sync Repo class uses the global runtime to bridge async-to-sync.
KnowledgeModel implements Queryable.
The error hierarchy is part of the binding layer.
KnowledgeModel is the primary class in the Python integration, wrapping all spec analysis operations.
The Repo API is the other main class in the Python integration, wrapping VCS operations.
Repo.import_xml and Repo.export_xml rely on stable, kind-separated leaf identities in the underlying repository DAG.
Repository leaf identities are represented as ContentHash values in the public API.
Repository branch and remote operations exposed by the CLI must preserve local working-copy edits while moving repository refs and trees.
Working-copy safety compares on-disk XML imports against recorded ContentHash values before allowing destructive writes.
Status, diff, and destructive-operation guards share the same tracked working-copy model and classification of local file changes.
Module structure defines how the Python package is organized into importable submodules.
The Queryable protocol unifies KnowledgeModel and Repo query surfaces within the integration.
The API section documents the KnowledgeModel class.
The shared query section documents the Queryable protocol.

art:mapping.art:note: The #[pymodule] root wires all submodules together: errors, query, spec types, KnowledgeModel, xml, repo.
RepoInner enum (Memory/Sqlite variants), dispatch! macro, global tokio runtime via OnceLock, and SharedRepo type alias.

spec:clayers.art:exempt: Documentation section containing py:exception elements. Individual exceptions are artifact-mapped.
Documentation section describing the Queryable protocol. The QueryResult py:class is artifact-mapped.
Vocabulary term defining the Queryable protocol concept. The implementing code is mapped via py:class and py:method nodes.
spec:clayers.llm:node: The Python integration section describes the clayers-py crate: a PyO3 native extension that wraps all four Rust crates (clayers-xml, clayers-spec, clayers-repo, and CLI-level logic) as the "clayers" Python package. The binding provides KnowledgeModel for spec analysis, sync/async Repo for VCS operations, ContentHash for hash manipulation, and a typed exception hierarchy. Key design: enum dispatch for generic Repo<S>, shared global tokio runtime for sync, Queryable protocol unifying KnowledgeModel and Repo.

Module layout: clayers (re-exports), clayers.xml (ContentHash), clayers.repo (sync Repo, MemoryStore, SqliteStore, Author), clayers.repo.aio (async Repo). Native extension is clayers._clayers. Users swap sync/async by changing the import path only.

Describes the KnowledgeModel class using structured py: elements. py:class with py:property (name, files, combined_xml, schema_dir) and py:method (validate, check_drift, coverage, connectivity, query, fix_node_hashes, fix_artifact_hashes). Each method has typed params and return types. Prose examples show CI gates, queries, coverage thresholds, and connectivity exploration.

Describes the Repo API using structured py: elements. py:class for Repo (12 methods: import/export, trees, commits, branches, tags, log, diff, query) plus supporting py:class elements for Author, CommitObject, FileChange, ContentHash. Sync and async share the same signatures; switching is import-path only.

Repository leaf objects avoid cross-kind hash aliasing. Text leaves retain the legacy raw-payload hash domain for compatibility, while comment and processing-instruction leaves hash tagged non-text byte domains. The non-text encoding is prefix "clayers-repo:leaf:v1\0", kind tag, NUL separator, then each payload part as u64 big-endian length plus bytes. Comments encode one part (content); PIs encode two parts (target and data, with missing data as empty). Equal payload strings in different XML node kinds therefore do not collide, and length-prefixing prevents ambiguous PI target/data splits. Import uses these hash helpers when constructing leaf objects; export regression tests verify that mixed content such as text "x" plus comment "x" round-trips without one object replacing the other in the store.

Checkout, orphan checkout, and pull are destructive working-tree update operations: they remove files from the previous tree and write files from the target tree. They must fail before changing the working tree when the repository has staged changes, unstaged tracked modifications, or unstaged tracked deletions. The export path also refuses to overwrite or remove an existing untracked file at a path owned by the target operation. Dirty detection uses the CLI working_copy table's stored document hashes and re-imports on-disk XML to compare current content against those hashes. Pull performs the guard before remote ref synchronization so a rejected pull does not advance refs while leaving the disk unchanged.

Status and no-argument diff compare the working_copy table with XML files on disk. They must not only scan files that still exist: tracked paths from working_copy are authoritative for detecting manual deletions. Status groups manual tracked deletions with other unstaged changes and keeps untracked XML files in the untracked section. Working-copy diff emits deleted entries for missing tracked files, modified entries for tracked files whose current import hash differs from the recorded hash, and added entries for untracked XML files. Added and deleted file entries do not need element-level XML diffs.
Describes the exception hierarchy using py:exception elements. ClayersError (base, extends Exception) with XmlError, SpecError, RepoError subclasses. Each maps to a Rust crate error type.

Describes the Queryable protocol and QueryResult using py: elements. py:class QueryResult with properties kind, count, values. The Queryable typing.Protocol unifies KnowledgeModel and Repo query APIs.
The clayers-py crate: PyO3 + maturin native extension. Wraps all Rust crates as Python classes. Module name: clayers._clayers. Pure Python wrappers provide re-exports and protocols.

Python class for loaded specs. Constructor discovers index/spec files. Methods: validate, check_drift, coverage, connectivity, fix_node_hashes, fix_artifact_hashes, query. All sync (clayers-spec is sync Rust).
typing.Protocol unifying KnowledgeModel and Repo query APIs. Single method: query(xpath, *, mode="xml") -> QueryResult. Runtime-checkable via isinstance().
Enum dispatch for PyO3: RepoInner::Memory(Repo<MemoryStore>) or RepoInner::Sqlite(Repo<SqliteStore>). Wrapped in Arc for sharing between sync/async classes. dispatch! macro routes calls.
OnceLock<tokio::runtime::Runtime> shared across all sync Repo instances. Multi-thread runtime with enable_all(). Sync methods call py.detach() to release GIL, then block_on() on this runtime.

art:mapping.art:note: Rust module root organizing repo submodules (sync, async, inner, objects) that implement the Python Repository API.
Typed leaf hash helpers for text, comment, and processing instruction leaves.
Import usage for kind-separated leaf hashes when constructing text, comment, and processing-instruction objects.
Regression coverage for equal text/comment payloads in mixed content round-trips.
Dirty working-copy guard that checks staged changes and unstaged tracked modifications/deletions against stored document hashes.
Checkout invokes the dirty working-copy guard before branch creation or switching.
Pull invokes dirty and target-tree working-copy guards before remote ref synchronization.
Working-copy export refuses to remove or overwrite dirty tracked files and untracked files at target paths.
Regression tests proving checkout and pull fail without overwriting staged or unstaged tracked edits, or untracked target files.
Status compares tracked working-copy paths with on-disk XML paths to report modified, deleted, and untracked local files.
Working-copy diff compares tracked paths and on-disk XML paths to emit deleted, modified, and added file entries.
Regression tests proving manual tracked deletions and untracked XML additions appear in status and working-copy diff output.
KnowledgeModel pyclass: wraps spec discovery, validation, drift, coverage, connectivity, hash fixing, and XPath querying.
PyO3 module root: registers KnowledgeModel, Repo, error types, and submodules as a Python native extension.
get_runtime() with OnceLock: lazily-initialized shared tokio multi-thread runtime for sync Repo class.
Rust-side module registration that defines the Python package structure. Python __init__.py files provide re-exports.
KnowledgeModel implementation: constructor, properties (name, files, schema_dir, combined_xml), and methods (validate, check_drift, coverage, connectivity, query, fix_*_hashes).

trm:term.trm:name: Store Compliance
trm:term.trm:definition: A test suite that verifies whether a Python store implementation correctly satisfies the ObjectStore, RefStore, and QueryStore trait contracts. The suite re-uses the same deterministic, property-based, and query tests that validate the built-in Rust backends, exercising them through the PyStore bridge against any user-provided Python class.

trm:term.trm:name: PyStore
trm:term.trm:definition: A Rust struct that implements the ObjectStore, RefStore, and QueryStore async traits by delegating every method call to a Python object through the GIL. Each trait method acquires the GIL via Python::attach, invokes the corresponding Python method, converts the result between StoreObject and the Rust Object enum, and releases the GIL. Transactions are bridged via a companion PyTransaction struct. The subtree method uses a built-in DAG walk via get() rather than requiring the Python store to implement streaming.

trm:term.trm:name: Store Object Types
trm:term.trm:definition: A set of frozen PyO3 classes that mirror every variant of the Rust Object enum: StoreElementObject, StoreTextObject, StoreCommentObject, StorePIObject, StoreDocumentObject, StoreTreeObject, StoreCommitObject, StoreTagObject, plus StoreAttribute, StoreAuthor, NamespaceDecl, and StoreTreeEntry. Each type supports bidirectional conversion between Python and Rust via From and to_rust() methods. The StoreObject wrapper provides a kind discriminator and typed accessor methods (as_element, as_text, etc.).

trm:term.trm:name: ComplianceMemoryStore
trm:term.trm:definition: A Python class backed by the Rust MemoryStore that implements the full store protocol (get, contains, transaction, get_by_inclusive_hash, get_ref, set_ref, delete_ref, list_refs, cas_ref, query_document). Used as the reference implementation for validating that the compliance test runner and the PyStore bridge work correctly. Since it delegates to a known-good Rust backend, any test failure indicates a bridge defect rather than a store bug.

trm:term.trm:name: Store Protocol
trm:term.trm:definition: A set of Python typing.Protocol classes that define the contract a custom store must implement: ObjectStore (get, contains, transaction, get_by_inclusive_hash), RefStore (get_ref, set_ref, delete_ref, list_refs, cas_ref), QueryStore (query_document), Transaction (put, commit, rollback), and the combined Store protocol. All are runtime_checkable. Users implement these protocols to create custom backends (e.g., Cloudflare Workers, HTTP proxies) and validate them with the compliance suite.

pr:section.pr:title: Store Compliance Testing
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: A Python-accessible test suite that exercises the full store contract against any Python-implemented backend.
pr:section.pr:p: Alternative repository backends (such as Cloudflare Workers with Durable Objects, or HTTP proxy stores) need a way to prove they correctly implement the ObjectStore, RefStore, and QueryStore contracts. The compliance tester bridges the existing Rust test battery to Python: users write a Python class implementing the store protocols, and the suite runs 129 tests against it: 49 deterministic store tests, 21 property-based tests via proptest (256 cases each), 9 deterministic concurrency tests, 3 property-based concurrency tests (32 cases each, bounded task counts), and 47 query tests.

The deterministic suite covers basic put/get/contains/rollback, full DAG roundtrip across all object variants, eight subtree topology cases (document, commit, diamond DAG, tag, mixed content, missing, empty, tree variants), seven transaction lifecycle edges (empty commit, drop without commit, tx interleaving, batch puts, idempotent within-tx puts, post-rollback recovery, pre-commit visibility), nine ref name pathology cases (Unicode, long names, special characters, prefix overlap, empty/no-match prefix listing, dangling pointers, delete idempotence, CAS with same expected/new), nine object content variants (octopus merges, namespace combinations, multi-PI prologues, tag chains, empty/large text, multiline comments, dataless PIs, bare elements), and four subtree consumer scenarios (deep chains, wide elements, mid-stream drop safety, partial consumption).

The concurrency suite spawns N=4-16 tokio tasks against a shared Arc<S> on a multi-threaded runtime. It verifies that exactly one CAS wins under contention (both create and swap paths), that set_ref races leave the ref in one of the input values, that idempotent puts are stable under concurrent writers, that distinct concurrent puts and transactions are all visible afterwards, that concurrent subtree readers agree on a snapshot, that disjoint refs are not cross-contaminated, and that readers during a writer never observe torn values. Property tests use 32 cases (instead of the 256 used for sequential properties) to keep total runtime bounded.

The architecture has four layers. First, store object types provide frozen PyO3 classes for every Object variant with bidirectional Rust/Python conversion. Second, the PyStore bridge implements the Rust store traits by calling back into Python through the GIL. Third, the compliance runner creates a fresh PyStore from the user's factory for each test, runs the test inside catch_unwind (for deterministic tests) or proptest's TestRunner (for property tests), and collects structured pass/fail results. Fourth, the Python compliance module provides protocol classes and a pytest integration layer via the compliance_tests() class factory.

pr:section.pr:title: Object Type Bridge
pr:section.pr:p: The py_objects module defines 12 PyO3 classes corresponding to every node in the Merkle DAG object model. Each class is #[pyclass(frozen)] with #[pyo3(get)] field access and a #[new] constructor. Rust-to-Python conversion uses From implementations; Python-to-Rust uses to_rust() methods that return Result for types containing timestamps (CommitObject, TagObject parse ISO 8601 strings back to DateTime). The StoreObject enum wrapper carries a kind discriminator string and typed accessor methods (as_element, as_text, as_comment, as_pi, as_document, as_tree, as_commit, as_tag).

pr:section.pr:title: PyStore Bridge
pr:section.pr:p: PyStore holds a Py<PyAny> pointing to a Python store instance and implements ObjectStore, RefStore, and QueryStore via #[async_trait]. Each trait method acquires the GIL through Python::attach, calls the corresponding Python method by name, extracts StoreObject or ContentHash results, and converts them to Rust types. Errors from Python (PyErr) are mapped to Error::Storage(msg). The subtree method replicates the subtree_walk DAG traversal algorithm rather than requiring the Python store to implement streaming. Transactions are bridged via PyTransaction, which delegates put, commit, and rollback through the same GIL callback pattern.

pr:section.pr:title: Compliance Runner
pr:section.pr:p: The run_store_compliance function accepts a Python callable (store factory) and returns a list of ComplianceResult objects. For each test, it calls the factory to get a fresh store, wraps it in PyStore, constructs a StoreTester or QueryTester, and runs the test method. Deterministic tests use catch_unwind around block_on to capture panics as failures. Property tests use proptest's TestRunner with 256 cases to avoid panic-based control flow across the FFI boundary. The GIL is released for the bulk of test execution via py.detach(); PyStore methods re-acquire it as needed for individual Python calls.

The ComplianceMemoryStore class provides a reference implementation for self-testing. It wraps the Rust MemoryStore and implements every store protocol method by delegating to the Rust backend via tokio::task::block_in_place and Handle::current().block_on(). This avoids nested runtime creation since the compliance runner already has an active tokio runtime.

pr:section.pr:title: Python Store Protocols
pr:section.pr:p: The clayers.compliance package exports five typing.Protocol classes: Transaction (put, commit, rollback), ObjectStore (get, contains, transaction, get_by_inclusive_hash), RefStore (get_ref, set_ref, delete_ref, list_refs, cas_ref), QueryStore (query_document), and the combined Store. All are @runtime_checkable. The compliance_tests(store_factory) function generates a Python class whose test methods each call run_store_compliance and assert individual results, enabling pytest to report each of the 88 Rust tests as a separate test case.

org:concept.org:purpose: Enable validation of custom Python store backends against the same contract that governs the built-in Rust implementations.

org:reference.org:purpose: Define the method signatures and type contracts that custom store implementations must satisfy.

rel:relation.rel:note: The compliance tester exercises the same store traits that the repository API wraps, bridging them to Python through PyStore.
The PyStore bridge and compliance runner require typed Python wrappers for all Object variants to marshal data across FFI.
PyStore is the core mechanism that makes compliance testing possible by bridging Python store method calls to Rust trait methods.
Object types provide the data conversion layer required by the PyStore bridge.
The runner orchestrates test execution, factory invocation, and result collection.
Protocols define the Python-side contract and pytest integration layer.

spec:clayers.llm:node: Store compliance testing enables validation of custom Python store backends against the clayers repository contract. It bridges 129 existing Rust tests (49 deterministic, 21 property-based via proptest, 9 deterministic concurrency, 3 property-based concurrency, 47 query) to Python through the PyStore bridge. Users implement Python Protocol classes (ObjectStore, RefStore, QueryStore, Transaction) and run the compliance suite via run_store_compliance() or pytest integration. Concurrency tests run on a multi-threaded tokio runtime with N tokio tasks racing on a shared Arc<S>; property concurrency tests use reduced case counts (32 vs 256) and bounded task counts (2-8) to keep runtime tractable. The ComplianceMemoryStore provides a reference implementation backed by the Rust MemoryStore for self-testing the bridge.

PyStore is a Rust struct implementing ObjectStore, RefStore, and QueryStore by delegating to a Python object via GIL callbacks. Each async trait method acquires the GIL, calls the Python method, converts StoreObject/ContentHash between Python and Rust types, and releases the GIL. Subtree uses a built-in DAG walk via get(). PyTransaction bridges write operations similarly.

Twelve frozen PyO3 classes wrapping every variant of the Rust Object enum: StoreElementObject, StoreTextObject, StoreCommentObject, StorePIObject, StoreDocumentObject, StoreTreeObject, StoreCommitObject, StoreTagObject, plus StoreAttribute, StoreAuthor, NamespaceDecl, and StoreTreeEntry. Support bidirectional conversion via From (Rust to Python) and to_rust() (Python to Rust). StoreObject provides a kind discriminator and typed accessors.

Five Python typing.Protocol classes defining the store contract: Transaction (put/commit/rollback), ObjectStore (get/contains/transaction/ get_by_inclusive_hash), RefStore (get_ref/set_ref/delete_ref/list_refs/ cas_ref), QueryStore (query_document), and the combined Store. All are @runtime_checkable. The compliance_tests() factory generates pytest-compatible test classes.

art:mapping.art:note: All 12 PyO3 store object wrapper classes with bidirectional conversion.
PyStore and PyTransaction implementing store traits via Python GIL callbacks.

Compliance runner, ComplianceResult, ComplianceMemoryStore, ComplianceTransaction, and per-category orchestrators (run_deterministic_tests, run_property_tests, run_concurrency_tests, run_concurrency_property_tests, run_query_tests).

StoreTester implementing 49 deterministic tests across base (put/get/contains/rollback/CAS/list/delete/roundtrip), subtree topology (Cat E base), transaction lifecycle (Cat B), ref name pathology (Cat C), object content variants (Cat D), and subtree consumer behavior (Cat E extensions). Reused across Memory, SQLite, and WebSocket backends via the store_tests! macro.

ConcurrencyTester implementing 9 deterministic concurrency tests (CAS race winner uniqueness, set_ref last-write-wins, idempotent concurrent puts, distinct concurrent puts, concurrent transactions, concurrent subtree readers, independent ref isolation, reader during writer) plus 3 property concurrency tests (32 cases each). Spawns multi-threaded tokio tasks against a shared Arc<S>. Macros concurrency_tests! and prop_concurrency_tests! are reused across Memory, SQLite, and WebSocket backends.
Python Protocol classes, compliance_tests() factory, and run_compliance() convenience function.
